BIBLIOGRAPHY: The Spice Islands in Prehistory Archaeology in the Northern Moluccas, Indonesia
COMMENTS: Description: Golo lies about 60 m inland from the head of the beach, in a low coral cliff. Its present-day earth floor lies between 8 and 9 m above high tide level. Like Um Kapat Papo, Golo Cave is essentially a creation of underwater solution processes that must have occurred when the reef was still below sea level. It has a high and uneven ceiling approximately 3-4 m high. In extent, it measures about 12 m from drip-line to back wall, and occupies a similar width. In the rear of the cave there is a dark passage that runs about 50 m into the rock. Adjacent to this is a kind of limestone gallery, formed by a long horizontal alcove fronted by a series of free-standing stalactite columns. (Peter Bellwood) | Keywords: gebe | Alternative_Name: Gua Golo

COMMENTS: Description: There are remains of 2 sarcophagi located in the Uma Lawas subak which is the oldest subak in the village of Manuaba. This site is thought to have originated from the Great Stone Age (Megalithichum). Its existence is still shrouded in mystery due to a lack of supporting data. Because of its location in the middle of rice fields, the Nyungsung (Purified) Farmer Community at Batulusu Temple, Subak Uma Lawas Kaja. Tipe C merupakan sarkofagus berukuran besar, dengan panjang antara200-268 cm. Ciri lainnya berupa ada dua tonjolan di bidang samping/panjang wadah. Sarkofagus tipe ini berukuran besar, sehingga mayat dapat dikuburkandengan terbujur dan kemungkinan diisi lebih dari satu mayat. Tipe ini disebutjuga sebagai tipe Manuaba, karena sebagian besar ditemukan di daerah Manuaba (Kabupaten Gianyar) | Keywords: sarcophagus bali | Alternative_Name: Pura Batulusu

LOCALISATION: South Sulawesi | Lake Matano, Nuha, East Luwu Regency, South Sulawesi, Indonesia
BIBLIOGRAPHY: Caldwell, I. (2000). Land of Iron: The Historical Archeology of Luwu and the Cenrana Valley. Centre for Southeast Asian Studies, The University of Hull.
COMMENTS: Description: 600 BP + habitation. Rahampu’u 1 (Mo. ‘initial settlement’) 70 metres west of the lake. The major surface feature at Rahampu’u 1 is an elongated mound, approximately 50 cm high (presumably higher originally), traversing the site from northeast to southwest. Local people cannot recall when the mound was made. Peter Bellwood suggested it may be piled-up rakeout from old ironworking hearths in the area of the S6T5 square (where the very high density of finds should relate to in situ smelting debris). In accord with Bellwood’s suggestion, the S6B10 test pit in the mound produced the same materials as excavated in the other test pits, but at highly variable concentrations deposited in diffuse lenses. | Keywords: matano EMP sulawesi | Sub-cultural Period: Palaeometallic | Alternative_Name: Lake Matano

LOCALISATION: South Sulawesi | Nuha, East Luwu Regency, South Sulawesi, Indonesia
BIBLIOGRAPHY: Caldwell, I. (2000). Land of Iron: The Historical Archeology of Luwu and the Cenrana Valley. Centre for Southeast Asian Studies, The University of Hull.
COMMENTS: Description: 12th century plus smelting. The Nuha test pit cut through approximately 92 centimetres of the kind of iron-smelting deposit that the OXIS archaeologists had previously encountered at Kampung Matano. Finds included 25.6 kilograms of iron debris (iron ore, slag, etc.), 500 grams of charcoal, 3.2 kg of lumps of baked earth identified as tuyère fragments, a possible iron tuyère-tip, two other iron fragments, 59 earthenware sherds with accreted iron bloom, and 24 flaked chert artifacts. The flaked chert would probably have been associated with a local strike-a-light industry, as recorded ethnographically among the Toraja (Van Heekeren 1950), and, according to our informant, Abdullah Andi Mattoana, still practised near Matano. A charcoal sample from the lowest excavated unit containing charcoal and abundant smelting debris was submitted for dating, and calibrates to between the eleventh and the thirteenth centuries (Table 2-3). A second charcoal sample from the top of the smelting deposit dates to the seventeenth century or later. These dates thus indicate that ore was smelted at the site throughout much of the second millennium AD, confirming the prediction by OXIS of iron smelting by at least the fourteenth century. | Keywords: matano EMP sulawesi | Sub-cultural Period: Palaeometallic | Alternative_Name: Lake Matano

LOCALISATION: South Sulawesi | Ussu, Malili, East Luwu Regency, South Sulawesi, Indonesia
BIBLIOGRAPHY: Caldwell, I. (2000). Land of Iron: The Historical Archeology of Luwu and the Cenrana Valley. Centre for Southeast Asian Studies, The University of Hull.
COMMENTS: Description: Bola Merajae produced an essentially continuous occupation sequence spanning the last two millennia, with a peak between approximately the thirteenth and fifteenth centuries. Two test pits were excavated on opposite banks of the Malaula stream where it leaves the sacred forest reserve. The test pits were probably sited in a rubbish disposal sector of the Bola Merajae site, which would explain the earthen sherds and abundance of charcoal, combined with a total lack of keramik in a sequence which overlaps the period when high-fired ceramics were imported to Luwu. | Keywords: EMP Luwu Sulawesi | Sub-cultural Period: Palaeometallic | Alternative_Name: B. ‘great house’ or ‘noble house’

LOCALISATION: South Sulawesi | Sassa, Baebunta, North Luwu Regency, South Sulawesi 92965, Indonesia
BIBLIOGRAPHY: Caldwell, I. (2000). Land of Iron: The Historical Archeology of Luwu and the Cenrana Valley. Centre for Southeast Asian Studies, The University of Hull.
COMMENTS: Description: The densest archaeological remains have been recovered at the far south of the site, west of the present-day Sabbang Loang Islamic cemetery, immediately overlooking the northern bank of the Rongkong river. Here Willems excavated a cluster of 11 massive burial jars. In the soil covering the jars he recovered iron spearheads, barkcloth beaters, mortars, and earthenware sherds. OXIS’ excavations in the vicinity recovered fragments of several burial jars, 191 flaked stone artifacts, an iron spearhead, various other iron fragments, two polished stone artifacts resembling a pick and a prong, a phallus-shaped river boulder, and a large Indo-Pacific glass bead. A consistent series of radiocarbon dates places the main phase of this occupation in the early centuries AD | Keywords: EMP Luwu Sulawesi | Sub-cultural Period: Palaeometallic

COMMENTS: Description: The observation reveals that around this piece of land there are abundance of pottery fragments. The son in law of the owner of this land (Mr. Zakaria), found two jars with stone lid that contain only earth, one was broken and the other seems intact (see Fig. 2). The shape of the broken jar can still be seen, and the measurements are: body diameter 30 cm, rim thickness 3 cm, mouth (opening) diameter 24 cm, height of jar 51 cm, and the neck is 14 cm high. The lid is 35 cm long, 33 cm wide, and the thickness is 6 cm. The lid of the second jar is 26 cm long, 34 cm wide, 15 cm thick. Another inhabitant, Mr. Wenas Paelo (60 years old), which is a Tokei Muhale, gave us information that pottery fragments were often found on the rice ﬁeld areas when people open or ploughed it. He has also found a large jar once, which contains necklace made of beads, but he did not remember the exact location

LOCALISATION: South Sulawesi | Onondoa, Rampi, North Luwu Regency, South Sulawesi | Rampi
COMMENTS: Description: People call the statue “Moni”, and it is believed to be the statue of a female ﬁgure. According to the locals, in 1990s a foreigner had tried to be lifted this statue on the grounds of protection. But the attempt failed. Instead, the statue sunk even deeper. The height of the statue from the surface is about 60 cm, its width is about 65 cm, and its length is 70 cm. It faces southwest.

LOCALISATION: South Sulawesi | Onondoa, Rampi, North Luwu Regency, South Sulawesi | Rampi
COMMENTS: Description: The local people named this statue the statue of”Anak Moni”. It is 75 cm high at front and 48 cm at the back. The width of the rock 29 cm and the width of its shoulders is more or less 50 cm. Sekitar 29 cm, Panjang bahu sekitar 50 cm. Lebar bahu 50 cm. The height from head to neck is 58 cm, and from chin downward is around 15 cm. The width of its nose is 8 cm, while the diameter of its eyes is 7.5 cm

LOCALISATION: South Sulawesi | Onondoa, Rampi, North Luwu Regency, South Sulawesi | Rampi
COMMENTS: Description: The distribution of potsherds and ﬂat stone slabs, which are thought to be fragments of burial stone jars and their lids, were found in large amount along the foothpath of Mamilo Forest. The average thickness of the potsherds is between 1–2.3 cm. The Stone Slab 1 is 53 cm long, 46 cm wide, and 17 cm thick, while the Stone Slab 2 is 39 cm long, 26 cm wide, and 12.2 cm thick. It is probable that the Stone Slab 1 is the lid of a quite large stone jar, while the second slab is the lid of a medium-sized jar. According to the local inhabitants, this location was an ancient settlement. The Human Statue 4 was found at the bottom of Padang Vwali hill slope, and was broken into two, most likely because it was fall down the hill due to landslide. It is 142 cm high, 115 cm wide, and about 28 cm thick. Probably it was facing the northeast. On the broken part of its head there are ﬁve wavy indentation, which seem to form a hairstyle called dodangka/dangka (meaning pendulum). But it is unclear whether the wavy indentation located on the right or left sides of the head

LOCALISATION: South Sulawesi | Onondoa, Rampi, North Luwu Regency, South Sulawesi | Rampi
COMMENTS: Description: Only ﬁve potsherds were found around the area.

LOCALISATION: South Sulawesi | Onondoa, Rampi, North Luwu Regency, South Sulawesi | Rampi
COMMENTS: Description: The artifacts found there include pedestals where house piles were placed on and a mortar. Most of the pedestals are no longer in situ, probably due to human activities. They are in the form of unworked stones from the river. They are rectangular in shape, measuring 47–62 cm long and about 25–31 cm wide, placed at the distance of 2.0 m and 1.7 m from each other. Not far from the pedestals, about 320 m to the southwest, there is a roundish and ﬂat mortar made of diorite rock. It is 67 cm long, 62.5 cm wide, and 22 cm thick, with a 8 cm-deep round hole 16 cm in diameter. The color of its surface is green due to moss.

LOCALISATION: South Sulawesi | Onondoa, Rampi, North Luwu Regency, South Sulawesi | Rampi
COMMENTS: Description: In Rampi language, Pehaha’ means a person who makes bark cloth (bark-cloth maker). At this location there is an earth frotress, which extends from east to west, at the north end of the hill ridge. Its wall, which is about 1.5 m wide and 50 cm to 1 m high, was less visible because it was covered with shrubs. There are also pedestals along the ridge, most of which seem in situ so that it was not difﬁcult to reconstruct the size of the building. The pedestals are made of natural boulders half-buried on the ground forming a rectangular space. Their amount at each location are between four and six pedestals.

LOCALISATION: South Sulawesi | Dodolo, Rampi, North Luwu Regency, South Sulawesi 92964 | Rampi
COMMENTS: Description: In the olden days the human statue was called “lolihi” by the locals, but now it is better known as “kontara”. This statue is one of the two statues sculpted in human-like shapes, which were reported by Kruyt in 1938. The statue looks worn out, but parts like eyes, ears, nose, eyebrows, and borders of face can still be seen. It is a torso statue without arms and legs. On the right part of its head are 5 wavy indentations (Kruyt mentioned 6). According to Tokei Mohale, the 5 wavy identation seem to form a hair style called dodangka/dangka (pendulum).Itis oriented to the east (facing Timo’oni Site), while Kruyt mentioned that it is oriented to the southeast. The measurement of Human Statue 3 is 134 cm high, the width of its head, including the ears, is 81 cm, the height of its head from the crown to the chin is 84 cm and from the chin to the ground is 50 cm, the diameter of the eyes are 13 cm, the width of the lower body is 68 c, the head is 21 cm and 18 cm thick, and the lower body is 14 cm high. About 30 m from the statue, there is a half-circle shaped ﬂat stone, on the yard of Mr. Sakeus Aho. The location is between 02°06 29.8 Northern Latitude and 20°16 24.6 Eastern Longitude, at an elevation of 983 m above sea level. The raw material is like the material of the Human Statue 3, which is granite (biotite granite) (Fig. 12). According to information, the object was in upright position and therefore looked like a half circle. Based on this indication, the survey team assumed that probably this object was originally intended to be made into a human statue. The stone clearly shown traces of working. With the presence of three small holes (pit-marked), there is a possibility that the object was later used as an altar or other media, such as mortar to grind or pound grains. The ﬂat stone is 122 cm long, 82 cm wide, and about 23–30 cm thick. The ﬁrst hole has a diameter of 11 cm and a depth of 6 cm, the second hole has a diameter of 6.5 cm and a depth of 2.5 cm, and the third has a diameter of 4.5 cm and a depth of 1.5 cm.

LOCALISATION: South Sulawesi | Rampi, North Luwu Regency, South Sulawesi | Rampi
COMMENTS: Description: A human statue (Human Statue 5) was found at Bangko Village with some burial jars. The Human Statue 5, or “Wa t u We r a n i , ” is located at Tinanu (Reuwe) Hamlet, Bangko Village). This statue is situated on a hill. According to Bola Bangko there is setttlement at the surrounding area. The hill is about 500 m from Kororaa River (Wai Kororaa). The Human Statue 5 (Fig. 14) is a torso type statue (without arms and legs), and it faces the east. On the right top of its head are four wavy depressions that resemble a type of hairstyle named dodangka/dangka, which means pendulum. It is ±123 cm high and ±76 cm wide (shoulder to shoulder). Its head is 43 cm wide, and the lower body is about 14 cm thick. The diameter of its eyes is 5 cm and its nose is 8 cm. The breasts protrude with a diameter of about 8 cm. It is unclear whether it represents female or male ﬁgure. In front of the statue there is a sunken ﬂat stone, which function is undecided, whether it was in the same context with the statue or merely funtioned to keep the statue from slanting further forward. The size of the ﬂat stone in front of the statue is 63 cm long, 55 cm wide, and about 11 cm thick. About 145 cm to the northwest of the human statue is an upright stone that resembles a tombstone. It is 73 cm high and about 35 cm thick. The width of the upper part is ±29 cm and the lower part is about 31 cm. The raw material of the statue is granite (biotite granite). In his book Kruyt (1938) did not mention the Human Statue 5. He only mentioned that at tbe village there are natural rocks called watu wurani, which were worshipped by the people at that period, and he believed that the natural rocks were used in later period (after the period when Megalithic artifacts were made). There is a possibility that Human Statue No. 5 or the rocks called “Wa t u We r a n i ”were not discovered until later. That is why the local inhabitants at the time had no knowledge about the existence of the Human Statue No. 5.

LOCALISATION: South Sulawesi | Rampi, North Luwu Regency, South Sulawesi | Rampi
COMMENTS: Description: The stone jars were empty (Fig. 15). The diameter of the body is about 86 cm, the cavity is about 65 cm deep, with a thickness of between 0.7–1 cm, and the diameter of the mouth (opening) is ± 35 cm. From the fragments, it seems as though the burial jars are carinated and have rounded bottom. About 2.5 m from the burial jar is ﬂat stone slab, which is assumed to be the lid of the jar. It is 64 cm long, 54 cm wide, and 9 cm thick.

LOCALISATION: South Sulawesi | Onondoa, Rampi, North Luwu Regency, South Sulawesi | Rampi
COMMENTS: Description: From results of surveys the team found locations where natural rocks are still considered sacred by the community until today: (a) the rice ﬁelds of Hiwontu at Rante Hamlet, where Mnahi (Watu Mpoihi) and Watu Baula statues are located, (b) the rice ﬁelds of Luwa’a, Pongka Hamlet, where Watu Tinanu (a statue that is shaped like a chair). About 300 m from Watu Tinanu there are stone pedestals where pillars of a rumah tambi (traditional house) and a buho (a structure that functions as a storeroom or granary) were placed on, only two left at each place. All the stones found at Tedeboe Village are natural rocks with no trace of sculpting | Alternative_Name: Watu Mpoihi Watu Baula

LOCALISATION: South Sulawesi | Leboni, Rampi, North Luwu Regency, South Sulawesi | Rampi
COMMENTS: Description: The team found some beads and lithic tools (ﬂakes), as well as ﬂat rounded stones among the distribution of potsherds. There is a great probability that this area was the location of burial jars. Those ﬁnds were found around pathways and waterways/canals with earth outcrops at Pada’ Powoliaha’a, on a sloping hilly meadow, about 2 km south- east of Beloi Hamlet that is the centre of Leboni Village.

LOCALISATION: South Sulawesi | Leboni, Rampi, North Luwu Regency, South Sulawesi | Rampi
COMMENTS: Description: There is also an arrangement of stones in Leboni Village, which area is considered sacred. The stone arrangement area was used as a sacred place to worship and perform rituals such as asking for rain, initiating the planting activities in the rice ﬁelds, or during certain occasions.

LOCALISATION: West Papua | Arguni, Kokas, Fakfak Regency, West Papua
COMMENTS: Description: Survey findings on the cave's surface revealed pottery fragments and mollusk shells, while faint images of palms and geometric shapes adorned the walls. | Keywords: papua, west papua, fakfak | Alternative_Name: Gua Dudumunir

LOCALISATION: West Papua | Arguni, Kokas, Fakfak Regency, West Papua
COMMENTS: Description: The figures observed on the Matutuo Arguni cliffs include humans, lizards, geometric shapes, and indistinct shapes. | Keywords: papua, west papua, fakfak

LOCALISATION: West Papua | Arguni, Kokas, Fakfak Regency, West Papua
COMMENTS: Description: There are red and yellow images with diverse shapes and styles. Depicting a series of "stories," most images are damaged or peeling. The images feature palms, geometric shapes, lizards, red dots, circles, turtles, fish, anthropomorphic figures, and fragments of human bones. | Keywords: papua, west papua, fakfak

LOCALISATION: West Papua | Arguni, Kokas, Fakfak Regency, West Papua
COMMENTS: Description: images with red and yellow hues reveal depictions of palms, circles, anthropomorphic figures, and geometric shapes. | Keywords: papua, west papua, fakfak

LOCALISATION: West Papua | Mambuniibuni, Kokas, Fakfak Regency, West Papua
COMMENTS: Description: At the cave mouth on the eastern side lies a mound of mollusk shells resembling a hill, while the western wall features a weathered image resembling a hand's palm. The Andarewa Cave comprises interconnected passages and surface discoveries of pottery fragments, mollusks, as well as human and animal bones, indicate its past use as a residential area. | Keywords: papua, west papua, fakfak | Alternative_Name: Gua Andarewa

LOCALISATION: West Papua | Goras, Kokas, Fakfak Regency, West Papua
COMMENTS: Description: The images identified on the Afofo site depict humans, palms, and soles. Arms, wrists, feet, reptiles, boomerangs, fish, geometric shapes, circles, combs, lines, and interconnected symbols. Hand drawing depicts a hand's shape with truncated fingers and a palm resembling that of a child. Flat and towering cliffs with a brownish hue are visible, featuring a red palm depicted on the perpendicular wall. Below the cliff, a series of red dots are arranged in a row. Positioned at the lower right corner of the circular image are the palm and soles of upward-facing arms. Additionally, various images of fish, geometric shapes, rectangles and circles are present. | Keywords: papua, west papua, fakfak

LOCALISATION: West Papua | Goras, Kokas, Fakfak Regency, West Papua
COMMENTS: Description: This location features paintings on extremely steep coral walls, with some showing signs of wear, all of which are red. | Keywords: papua, west papua, fakfak

LOCALISATION: West Papua | 7JFQ+HRG Furir, Fakfak Regency, West Papua
COMMENTS: Description: This location is more commonly referred to as Putri Island, believed to be the resting place of mermaids according to local legend. This belief is supported by the existence of graves on the cliff terraces, along with rows of skulls and remnants of human bones. Additionally, remnants such as wooden boards, paddles, and tifa can be found. The cliff structure features a distinctive white indentation, with wooden stairs leading to the top. Faded red images of a palm tree and a fish can be observed on various parts of the walls. At the edge of the towering cliff, enveloped by verdant trees, a crimson mark catches the eye. Initially resembling a red smudge, closer inspection reveals it to be a palm impression. The image is situated 15 meters above sea level. | Keywords: papua, west papua, fakfak | Alternative_Name: Pulau Putri

LOCALISATION: West Papua | 7JFQ+H8H Furir, Fakfak Regency, West Papua
COMMENTS: Description: The site also highlights red artworks, although the theme is somewhat ambiguous, only discernible through red paint on the extremely steep rock surfaces. | Keywords: papua, west papua, fakfak

LOCALISATION: West Papua | 7JFQ+H8H Furir, Fakfak Regency, West Papua
COMMENTS: Description: East of Furir village lies a promontory featuring walls shaped like a brownish-white niche. Atop the terrace sits a tomb structure surrounded by stacks of plates and skull bones swathed in white cloth. Adorning the walls and ceiling are red paintings. The images that astonish us are located in an area measuring 34 meters in length and 2-4 meters in width. In addition to the presence of red, yellow is also visible. The images discovered at the Sora'a site include depictions of palms, arms, feet, rectangles, geometric shapes, flower petals, spears, fish, lizards, jellyfish, humans, red circles, dots, and anthropomorphic figures. Some images are damaged and challenging to recognize. The predominant image features a palm alongside geometric shapes. | Keywords: papua, west papua, fakfak

LOCALISATION: West Papua | 7JFQ+H8H Furir, Fakfak Regency, West Papua
COMMENTS: Description: Discoveries within the cave include pottery shards, animal bones, mollusks, and fragments of the ceiling. The cavern ceiling displays a darkened figure in a deteriorated state. The findings suggest the impact and practices of Austronesian culture that previously flourished in the Berau Bay region. | Keywords: papua, west papua, fakfak | Alternative_Name: Gua Sosoraweru

LOCALISATION: West Papua | 7JFQ+H8H Furir, Fakfak Regency, West Papua
COMMENTS: Description: Images of palms, wrists, arms, geometric shapes, fish, and boomerangs adorn the white cliff walls, accompanied by rows of red dots. | Keywords: papua, west papua, fakfak

LOCALISATION: West Papua | 8H5P+3HG Fior, Fakfak Regency, West Papua
COMMENTS: Description: The site is referred to as Mbosu'umata due to its location at the edge of the cliff. Stalactites bear a resemblance to pig snouts. Red images adorn the white cliff walls, alongside other indiscernible shapes. Human bone fragments are also present on the terrace. | Keywords: papua, west papua, fakfak

LOCALISATION: West Papua | 8G8R+M5M Fior, Fakfak Regency, West Papua
COMMENTS: Description: The website is predominantly featuring palm tree images. Additionally, there is a picture of Matutuo. It is worn, backfired, and has red dots. At a height of 20 meters, images of interlocking palms and geometric lines are visible. The site faces eastward, with perpendicular brownish-white cliffs, named for the bird pictures it hosts. Known as Wamaraim with white markings on the field. On the cliff, there are images of upright palms, turtles, and lizards. Additionally, there is an image of a palm tree with an upward orientation. Additionally, there are fish shapes, dots, and various images with unidentified shapes. Furthermore, there is an image resembling an elongated piece of wood with a slightly pointed end. | Keywords: papua, west papua, fakfak

LOCALISATION: West Papua | 8FJW+4F5 Andamata, Fakfak Regency, West Papua
COMMENTS: Description: The cliff's condition manifests as a crevasse that directly overlooks the sea. The distance of the sea level at low tide is 4 meters. Pictures adorn the walls and ceilings. Images are arranged in clusters of different shapes and sizes, predominantly in red with a yellow hue. The cliff displays various images such as red dots, hands, boomerangs, geometric shapes, stripes, shields, lizards, fish, curved lines, circles, combs, and crescent moons, alongside some more obscure depictions. | Keywords: papua, west papua, fakfak

LOCALISATION: West Papua | 8FJW+4F5 Andamata, Fakfak Regency, West Papua
COMMENTS: Description: The cliff's condition manifests as a crevasse that directly overlooks the sea. The distance of the sea level at low tide is 4 meters. Pictures adorn the walls and ceilings. The cliff displays various images such as red dots, hands, boomerangs, geometric shapes, stripes, shields, lizards, fish, curved lines, circles, combs, and crescent moons, alongside some more obscure depictions. | Keywords: papua, west papua, fakfak

LOCALISATION: West Papua | Kaimana Kota, Kaimana, Kaimana Regency, West Papua | West Papua,Kaimana
COMMENTS: Description: The rock image is on a karst cliff with a height of about 20 meters and facing east. From the identification results, there is a picture shaped like a fish, then 3 anthropomorphic patterned pictures and 26 dots. | Keywords: papua, west papua, Kaimana | Alternative_Name: Tanjung Bitcari

LOCALISATION: West Papua | Kaimana Kota, Kaimana, Kaimana Regency, West Papua | West Papua,Kaimana
COMMENTS: Description: This site is located on a cliff facing east and is in the coastal area. The height of the cliff is estimated to be around 8.6 meters, while the picture of the rock is in the upper niche. It is quite difficult to identify the shape of the rock image, because the damage is quite severe and only leaves red spots from the image that have not been peeled off. | Keywords: papua, west papua, Kaimana | Alternative_Name: Tanjung Bitcari

LOCALISATION: West Papua | Kaimana Kota, Kaimana, Kaimana Regency, West Papua | West Papua,Kaimana
COMMENTS: Description: Unfortunately, the image of a rock in the form of a niche on a cliff with a height of about 8 meters is very difficult to identify, because it has suffered severe damage. | Keywords: papua, west papua, Kaimana | Alternative_Name: Tanjung Bitcari

LOCALISATION: West Papua | Kotdol, Serambakon, Pegunungan Bintang Regency, Papua
COMMENTS: Description: Tempayan Stone is a natural stone that is not modified by humans, in the shape of a half circle like an inverted bowl, is in the sea near the cliff beach, some of the rock bodies are embedded in the water and others appear to the surface in the form of a half circle. In the part of the stone observed above the water surface has a condition that begins to be fragile and cracked | Keywords: papua, west papua

LOCALISATION: West Papua | Ormuwari, Ravenirara, Jayapura Regency, Papua
COMMENTS: Description: Stone seats are made of monoliths that are moulded to form a chair that has a backrest and a seat that is united. The seat functions as a seat for Ondoafi and a symbol of Ondoafi's greatness. Ondoafi is a leader who acts as a protector, welfare, and preserver of a united small world life .(Griapon 2014, 2) | Keywords: papua, west papua | Alternative_Name: Batu kursi

LOCALISATION: West Papua | Ormuwari, Ravenirara, Jayapura Regency, Papua
COMMENTS: Description: Tajaho stone is in the form of natural stone without any work at all and has a shape like a gurango fish or hammer shark. Batuini functions as a sign of the house (former house) or the sign of the location of the Toto II Tribe's house, and evidence of ownership of customary territory recognised by other surrounding tribes. | Keywords: papua, west papua | Alternative_Name: Batu Tahajo

LOCALISATION: West Papua | Ormuwari, Ravenirara, Jayapura Regency, Papua
COMMENTS: Description: Ancestor Stone are two monolithic stones that are believed to be the ancestors of the Nari Tribe descended from Ondoafi that turned into stones. The two stones are natural stones without processing. The longer stone shows the male ancestor (Tandiriko) and the shorter and slightly round one shows the female ancestor (Mandaarki). The Ancestor stone functions as a unifying symbol of the Trong Tribe from the Nari clan descended from Ondoafi. This stone is also evidence of ownership of traditional territory. | Keywords: papua, west papua

LOCALISATION: West Papua | Wambena, Depapre, Jayapura Regency, Papua
COMMENTS: Description: The Yendaepiwai stone is in the form of four monoliths lying horizontally in the yard of Mr. Matthew Yerisitauow. The distance of the first stone to the second stone is 5 metres, the second stone to the third stone 4 metres, and the third stone to the fourth stone 2.20 metres. The stones have various sizes. On the surface of the stone there are a number of holes and cracks that are believed to be the scars of spears and arrows. Not far from the location of Batu Yendaepiwai, there is Batu Permen, which is a very large natural stone that lies like hanging on the coast. | Keywords: papua, west papua | Alternative_Name: Batu Yendaepiwai 1-4

LOCALISATION: West Papua | Wambena, Depapre, Jayapura Regency, Papua
COMMENTS: Description: The Yendaepiwai stone is in the form of four monoliths lying horizontally in the yard of Mr. Matthew Yerisitauow. The distance of the first stone to the second stone is 5 metres, the second stone to the third stone 4 metres, and the third stone to the fourth stone 2.20 metres. The stones have various sizes. LOCALISATION: West Papua | HJ22+CQH Nehibe, Jayapura Regency, Papua
COMMENTS: Description: Sukun Stone is a number of stones of various sizes and shapes that are arranged in an area of 2,60 metres x 2,60 metres. These stones consist of a mother stone in the form of a monolith shaped like a diamond or a parallelogram with an uneven surface. In addition, there is a menhir statue with an oval head shape and wearing a tapered head and a circular headband on the forehead. On his oval face, there is a rope tie on the edge of the face from the forehead to the chin. His eyes and nose are worn out so they are invisible, his ears wear wavy earrings that jump up to his shoulders, the tip of his chin is long to his chest and covers his neck. The chest of the statue is not identified, but it seems to be using clothes because on the arm there are grooves like dangling cloth. His waist is wearing a waistband. On the genital part, there is a triangle shape, at the bottom there is a boundary in the form of a circular line, while on the side and back of the statue there are grooves of clothes that fall like fabric grooves under the neck and waist. The shape of the leg is unknown because the condition of the lower part is tapered, maybe because it is worn out or it is. Another possibility is because the position of the statue in the past was not on his back or sleeping on the ground, but was stuck standing on the ground. According to the community, this statue is a girl (Merawai) who is the source of the sukun fruit. Besides this, there are eight oval-shaped menhirs with smooth surfaces arranged transversely on the ground surface with a west-north-south east orientation. There is also a stone plate (betew) that has been broken and the broken part is gone. On the remaining stone plates, three oval-shaped basins appear on the surface. One of the basins is still intact, while the other two basins are partially left because they are broken. The stone plate functions as a place for offerings. | Keywords: papua, west papua | Alternative_Name: Batu Sukun

LOCALISATION: West Papua | Meukisi, Yokari, Jayapura Regency, Papua 99354
COMMENTS: Description: Lingkar Stone (oru dia ) is a number of monoliths arranged in a circle. The arranged stones are generally round, oval, and flat with a smooth surface. The Ring Stone is in the yard of Ondoafi's house or the chiefs according to their respective roles in the custom. The Ring Stone Formation consists of eight stones, ten stones, eleven stones, and twelve stones. According to the community, the number of stones on each ring stone should be twelve according to the number of their descendants, and the middle part is a place to make fire. At this time, from a number of Lingkaryang Stones, some have been found that are arranged on the ground without protective buildings and some have also been given protective buildings. There is also a Ring Stone that has been cast so that it cannot be moved. Lingkardi Stone functions as a seat during customary deliberation. However, at this time in some areas of Batu Lingkar there are benches that are arranged in a circle for seating and the existence of Batu Lingkar is only as a symbol. As a place of deliberation, usually if a meeting is going to be held, it is given a code or sign by lighting a fireplace in the middle of the Ring Stone. The ring stones are in accordance with their function divided into two, namely for peaceful tribal groups who talk about the problems of daily life of indigenous people, both related to economy, socio-culture, health, and welfare. War tribal groups specialise in discussing war issues. In every village where there is a Lingkar Stone. The number of Ring Stones for a peaceful tribe is more than for a war tribe which is only one. | Keywords: papua, west papua | Alternative_Name: Batu Lingkar

LOCALISATION: West Papua | Endokisi, Yokari, Jayapura Regency, Papua 99354

The ring stones are in accordance with their function divided into two, namely for peaceful tribal groups who talk about the problems of daily life of indigenous people, both related to economy, socio-culture, health, and welfare. War tribal groups specialise in discussing war issues. In every village where there is a Lingkar Stone. The number of Ring Stones for a peaceful tribe is more than for a war tribe which is only one. | Keywords: papua, west papua | Alternative_Name: Batu Lingkar

LOCALISATION: West Papua | Snamai, Yokari, Jayapura Regency, Papua 99353
COMMENTS: Description: Lingkar Stone (oru dia ) is a number of monoliths arranged in a circle. The arranged stones are generally round, oval, and flat with a smooth surface. The Ring Stone is in the yard of Ondoafi's house or the chiefs according to their respective roles in the custom. The Ring Stone Formation consists of eight stones, ten stones, eleven stones, and twelve stones. According to the community, the number of stones on each ring stone should be twelve according to the number of their descendants, and the middle part is a place to make fire. At this time, from a number of Lingkaryang Stones, some have been found that are arranged on the ground without protective buildings and some have also been given protective buildings. There is also a Ring Stone that has been cast so that it cannot be moved. Lingkardi Stone functions as a seat during customary deliberation. However, at this time in some areas of Batu Lingkar there are benches that are arranged in a circle for seating and the existence of Batu Lingkar is only as a symbol. As a place of deliberation, usually if a meeting is going to be held, it is given a code or sign by lighting a fireplace in the middle of the Ring Stone. The ring stones are in accordance with their function divided into two, namely for peaceful tribal groups who talk about the problems of daily life of indigenous people, both related to economy, socio-culture, health, and welfare. War tribal groups specialise in discussing war issues. In every village where there is a Lingkar Stone. The number of Ring Stones for a peaceful tribe is more than for a war tribe which is only one. | Keywords: papua, west papua | Alternative_Name: Batu Lingkar

LOCALISATION: West Papua | Yaugapsa, Demta, Jayapura Regency, Papua
COMMENTS: Description: Somda Stone is a monolith believed to be a grandmother who has turned into stone. The location of Batu Somda is the place where the grandmother was killed in Kampung Tua Yapumara. That stone has become a historical stone in the life of the tribe living in the Demta region. They are tribes that used to be scattered to various regions, even reached the East Nusa Tenggara region (Timor Island), but they have been reunited. | Keywords: papua, west papua | Alternative_Name: Batu Somda

LOCALISATION: West Papua | Demta Kota, Demta, Jayapura Regency, Papua
COMMENTS: Description: The 'Tungku Api' Fire Furnace is a burning furnace located in the middle of a traditional meeting place. This furnace is in the shape of a circle of an array of stones that rising and the centre is filled with soil and ash from burning as well as a fireplace that is still burning. In the area around the furnace, there are long benches arranged around the furnace as seats during the deliberation. Fire in the furnace must die and must be kept alive. This is related to trust thanks to life and the presence of immortal figures (Wari). This Fire Furnace is the centre to talk about traditional issues such as genealogy, past life and future, social economy, life difficulties, time considerations, and community welfare. | Keywords: papua, west papua | Alternative_Name: Tungku Api

LOCALISATION: West Papua | Usaha Jaya, Misool Timur, Raja Ampat Regency, West Papua | Misool
COMMENTS: Description: The surface of the wall is uneven with niches that had developed due to the rock formation process and the growing bushes. The rock art was drawn on the niches on the wall surface, but some have started to fade, so careful and meticulous treatment is necessary. The results from the study identified 19 rock art motifs that consisted of 1 human figure motif, 1 human face motif, 1 turtle motif, 1 hand drawing motif, 1 boomerang motif, 1 drum motif, 2 geometric motif, 1 circle motif, 1 parallel line motif, and 9 unidentified motifs. There are at least 6 figurative category motifs and 13 non-figurative motifs with 8 line types of motifs, 3 outline types of motifs, 5 solid infill types of motifs, and 2 negative type of motifs. From the visualization aspect of the motifs, the brush technique was applied in 8 motifs and another 8 were sprayed using red colored substances. There were 8 identified hand motifs that consisted of 1 right hand drawing and 7 hand drawings that could not be recognized. Other parts of the hand depicted in the motifs consisted of 6 fingers up to the palm, 1 finger to the wrist, and 1 finger up to the elbow. The hand drawings are negative type and oriented upwards. The dimension of the cliff chamber is 25 meters long measured from end to end of the rock art, while the height of the rock art is measured from the sea level to the nearest rock art which is 15 meters a.m.s.l, facing the southeast. | Keywords: papua, west papua, misool

LOCALISATION: West Papua | Usaha Jaya, Misool Timur, Raja Ampat Regency, West Papua | Misool
COMMENTS: Description: One of the most interesting findings in this site is the rock art facing the south. The wall surface is uneven with cracks from the formation process and stalactites. However, some of the patterns on the coral surface and on the stalactites facing the south have faded, therefore, careful and meticulous handling is necessary to observe and examine the drawings. The rock art found in this site can be categorized into 16 objects namely: one image of a fish, one image of a bird, seven images of hand stencils, three geometrical images, but four other images were not identifiable. Apparently, there were six drawings that were made by using brush technique, while ten images used the spray technique. All the drawings were colored in red but nine drawings are categorized as figurative patterns and seven drawings are non-figurative, while five drawings are linier types, one drawing is a dotted type, one drawing is an outline type, one drawing as a solid infill type and seven drawings as a negative type. From the seven hand stencils that were identified, three were hand stencils of the left hand and two were right-hand stencils while the remaining two hand stencils could not be identified. One drawing only showed an image of one finger, and there were five hand stencils of the palm and one drawing showed the fingers until the elbow. The hand stencils in Muslim Cave were drawn on the cave walls and on the stalactites. | Keywords: papua, west papua, misool | Alternative_Name: Gua Muslim

LOCALISATION: West Papua | Usaha Jaya, Misool Timur, Raja Ampat Regency, West Papua | Misool
COMMENTS: Description: In this site, 184 rock drawings were identified that consisted of drawings of humans (4), anthropomorphic (2), fish (20), sea cucumbers (8), jelly fish (2), sea horses (4), turtles (2), lizards (1), snakes (1), four legged animals (1), fish tail (1), hand stencils (33), spears (1), arrows (5), axes (2), geometric shapes (23), circles (5), roundular shapes (15), patterned wheels (2), lines (1), parallel lines (3), and several other unidentified objects (52). Based on the types of the drawings, the rock art in this site consisted of 20 dotted-line drawings, 26 line drawings, 41 outline drawings, and 29 solid infill drawings, and 68 negative drawings. From the technique of the drawing, there were 95 brush drawings, and there were 88 drawings that used the spraying technique, consisting of images of hand stencils, axes, decorated wheels, sea horses, fish tails and geometrical shapes. In creating the hand stencils under the spraying technique, the hands were pressed against the wall and then sprayed with coloring. However, in making the fine and beautiful patterns of the decorated wheels, the creators used a certain molding that was placed on the wall and then it was sprayed with coloring. | Keywords: papua, west papua, misool

LOCALISATION: West Papua | Usaha Jaya, Misool Timur, Raja Ampat Regency, West Papua | Misool
COMMENTS: Description: The Dolphin Site is located 1o57’48.31” South Latitude and 130o27’34.27” East Longitude. In this site, there are nine patterns consisting of eight hand stencils and one fish drawing. There were two techniques applied in making the drawings, first the brush technique that was used in drawing the fish image and the second is the spray technique applied in making the hand stencils with red coloring and facing upward. The fish figures are solid infill drawings that depict dolphins positioned in between hand stencils facing upward. Meanwhile, the hand stencils are negative-type patterns and all of them are images of the right hand super-positioned (overlapping). The hand stencils were not decorated, and there were four objects only showing fingers and the palm and another four objects showing fingers up to the wrist. There were six drawings facing upward and two drawings facing the left or facing each other. The chamber in the cliff rock is ± 2 meters long measured from end to end of the drawings, while the height of the drawings measured from the sea level to the closest drawing is ± 3 meters above sea level, facing the west. The rock art in this site is in good condition. | Keywords: papua, west papua, misool

LOCALISATION: West Papua | Kibay, Arso Timur, Keerom Regency, Papua
COMMENTS: Description: Isisuk Cliff in local terms is called Isisuk Cave. This cave is in the traditional ownership of Kuntui and Psakor. This site is a sleeping house for local residents when travelling across the border (return) from Kampung Kibay to the Papua New Guinea region and vice versa. In this bedroom there are barriers for men and women. The part for women is called the birthing house. This barrier is characterised by a small hole on the cliff that resembles the footprints of a human foot. The footprint of this foot is one metre above the ground. Based on the story of the people, this footprint is the footprint of their ancestors. However, as a result of the observation of the research team, the footprint is a natural formation. Isisuk Cliff until now is still used by the Kibay community as a place to live when travelling both to Papua New Guinea and vice versa. A lot of vandalism was found on this cliff with graffiti in the form of pictures and people's names. The handwriting uses Roman letters. In addition to paintings, fragments of animal bones and animal teeth were also found. Based on the identification of the shape of Tebing Isisuk wall painting, the shape of the image can be classified consisting of figurative and non-figurative images and there are images that have faded and are difficult to identify the shape. In table 1 it can be seen that there are two figurative images, consisting of palms and soles, while five non-figurative images consist of one spiral image, one geometric image, and three abstract images can be seen in table 2. Figure 4 shows that the technique of making pictures on this site is done by drawing/drawing by hand. The colour of the picture is black, and the basic material is charcoal. | Keywords: papua, west papua

LOCALISATION: West Papua | Yuruf, Web, Keerom Regency, Papua
COMMENTS: Description: The distance between the cave and the river is seven metres. According to informant Derek Mandoweri, erfe hora is the name of a woman who was killed in a location near the cave. The woman's blood flowed into this cave, so the Yuruf (Indangan) people named this cave erfe hora. Erfe Hora Cave is located in the traditional area of Keret Watai. The direction facing the northeast cave. Based on the identification of the form of the Erfe Hora cave wall painting, it can be classified as a non-figurative form. There are also images of paintings that are faded and the shape is not identified. Table 3 and figure 5 show that the form of non figurative painting. On the wall of the Erfe Hora cave as many as eight pieces, consisting of lines and abstract circles. The placement of this picture is irregular, some are on the cave ceiling, the outer cave wall and rock crevices. The whole picture is black, using charcoal base material. Made with scratching technique, using hands | Keywords: papua, west papua | Alternative_Name: Gua Erfe Hora

LOCALISATION: West Papua | Yuruf, Web, Keerom Regency, Papua
COMMENTS: Description: Based on the identification of the form of the Yakumbru cave wall painting, figurative and non-figurative image forms can be classified. In table 6, the figurative image is in the form of a millipede, while in table 7 the non-figurative image is in the form of a circle (division). In figure 7 it is shown that figurative and non figurative images are black, using charcoal as a basic material. Made with scratching technique using hands. | Keywords: papua, west papua

LOCALISATION: West Papua | Yuruf, Web, Keerom Regency, Papua
COMMENTS: Description: Based on the identification of the shape of the Yahoto cave wall painting, the shape of the image can be classified as consisting of figurative and non-figurative images and there are images that are faded and difficult to identify the shape. In table 8 it can be seen that the figurative image shape there are six pieces, consisting of the soles of the feet, palms, human faces, and lizards (soa-soa), while in table 9 there are 18 non-figurative images, consisting of noken images, pig bones, circles, and abstracts. Figure 8 shows that figurative images are generally black, and non-figurative images are mixed in black and red. Black colour uses charcoal base material while red colour uses laterite. Laterite is a red coloured stone. This stone is located in the neighbourhood of the site or more precisely on the outskirts of Kali Mba. The drawing technique on this site is done by drawing/drawing by hand. | Keywords: papua, west papua

LOCALISATION: West Papua | CFJ4+R24 Doyo Lama, Jayapura Regency, Papua
BIBLIOGRAPHY: Papua Jurnal Penelitian Arkeologi Papua dan Papua Barat Vol.1 No.2 November 2009
COMMENTS: Description: Rock art: These paintings were inscribed on stone surface with scratch technique, there is some are painted singly and some are in pairs one boulder consists of 2 to 5 painting in the direction facing the painting is sufficient varies. The rock paintings are comprehensive into 4 fenced sectors and 3 sectors that have not yet been ring-fenced The total number of painting motifs is 147 paintings etched on chunks 115 stones. Stone chiselled figures (Pahatan Batu): At the Tutari megalithic site in sector IV there are several stones carved to resemble a creature carved to resemble a a creature consisting of a head, neck and body. These stones are believed to be the leaders of the cursed Tutari tribe, meanwhile the condition of some of these stones has begun eroded until it has its shape and size not the same anymore. Stone Rows (Jajaran Batu): Represent an arrangement of two rows of stones (The right row consists of 70 stones and the row on the left consists of 44 pieces stone) with the base oriented towards groups of painted stones and sections the tip leads to a group of menhirs. Models of megalithic remains like this are common associated with a ceremony activity. Stone Enclosure (Batu Temu Gelang): It is an arrangement of stones that are arranged circular one end to the other meet. the number of existing stones about 6 groups and is in a painted stone complex. As for form The arrangement of the temugelang stones is quite varied. Menhirs (Batu Tegak): On the peak of the Tutari hill it is found quite a lot of menhirs of that size varies, starting from the smallest size 14 em to the largest size of 88 em. There are a total of 110 groups of menhirs with its own uniqueness, namely that the menhirs are located at megalithic sites Tutari is not embedded into the ground however only erected supported by stones small so it doesn't collapse (Prasetyo, 1994). | Keywords: papua, west papua

LOCALISATION: Sumatra
Sumatra | Napal Melintang, Limun, Sarolangun Regency, Jambi | Hoabinhian,Neolithic
COMMENTS: Description: In 2019, three test pits were placed near the entrance (TP1, 150x150 cm and TP3, 150x100 cm) and the main chamber of the cave (TP2, 150x150 cm). Although the three test pits exhibit the existence of anthropogenic deposits, the densest archaeological findings only appear in TP1 and TP3 squares. Two perforated stones were found from TP3 square at a depth of 40-45 cm below the cave floor (Fauzi et al., 2019). These artifacts were made of slate, one of a fine-grained and relatively soft rock that is abundantly available in the rivers and creeks nearby the site. | Keywords: sumatra, hoabinh, neolithic | Sub-cultural Period: Palaeometallic | Alternative_Name: Gua Mesiu

LOCALISATION: Sumatra | Padang Bindu, Semidang Aji, Ogan Komering Ulu Regency, South Sumatra | Hoabinhian,Neolithic
COMMENTS: Description: Specimen GS1 was found in LU1 (Lubang Uji 1 or test pit 1) during the 2002 fieldwork. It was recorded as ‘batu bergores’ (incised/grooved stone) with inventory number SLB1/02/LU1/2256. The specimen was unearthed from a light brown sandy-clay layer (layer 2), at the depth of 77 cm below the cave floor. It was found together with potsherds and flake tools made of obsidian, chert, and andesite (Simanjuntak et al., 2006) | Keywords: sumatra, hoabinh, neolithic | Sub-cultural Period: Palaeometallic | Alternative_Name: SLB1

LOCALISATION: Sumatra | Padang Bindu, Semidang Aji, Ogan Komering Ulu Regency, South Sumatra | Hoabinhian,Neolithic
COMMENTS: Description: The paintings of parallel, circular, and wavy lines made by red pigment (possibly ochre) were found on the walls and ceiling. However, no absolute dating has been carried out on these paintings and thus, it is difficult to assign them to a specific cultural period. An attempt to date the paintings was made by describing the similarity of ‘chevron’ motifs to local pottery (Ansyori, 2016), which would make the paintings no older than the Neolithic period1 (Widianto et al., 2015). | Keywords: sumatra, hoabinh, neolithic | Sub-cultural Period: Palaeometallic | Alternative_Name: Gua Harimau

LOCALISATION: Sumatra | Maribung, Limun, Sarolangun Regency, Jambi | Hoabinhian,Neolithic
COMMENTS: Description: Sungai Lului Cave is located on the foot of Calau Petak Hill. The cave contains 32 clear images, consisting of black and greyish white linear, circular, geometric planes, zoomorphic figures (deer, centipede, and lizard), and anthropomorphic figures. In addition, there are 3 ex- tremely faded images and it leave only the traces of pigments with an amorphous design. The drawings are located approximately 0.8 m up to 2 m above the present cave floor. | Keywords: sumatra, hoabinh, neolithic | Sub-cultural Period: Palaeometallic | Alternative_Name: Gua Sungai Lului

LOCALISATION: Sumatra | Maribung, Limun, Sarolangun Regency, Jambi | Hoabinhian,Neolithic
COMMENTS: Description: Kerbau 1 Cave is located on the foot of Bulan Hill, surrounded by several other smaller caves. It has been used as a buffalo shed in recent times. In total, 10 images have been recorded and comprise anthro- pomorphic, zoomorphic, geometric lines and plans and several amor- phous images. They are made with a white colored pigment and located on the ceiling of the cave, approximately 11 m above the present cave floor. | Keywords: sumatra, hoabinh, neolithic | Sub-cultural Period: Palaeometallic | Alternative_Name: Gua Kerbau 1

LOCALISATION: Sumatra | Napal Melintang, Limun, Sarolangun Regency, Jambi | Hoabinhian,Neolithic
COMMENTS: Description: Kandang Kerbau 3 Cave is located on the foot of Calau Tengah Hill. As with Kerbau 1, it is used as a buffalo shed. In addition, the ceiling of some parts of the cave is coated with a thick layer of soot, as a result of fire from a modern hearth. The art in this cave consists of 7 images, comprising geometric lines, zoomorphic figure (bird?), and faded amorphous images. All of those images are drawn in black, and located approximately 2 m above the modern cave floor. | Keywords: sumatra, hoabinh, neolithic | Sub-cultural Period: Palaeometallic | Alternative_Name: Gua Kandang Kerbau 3

LOCALISATION: Sumatra | Napal Melintang, Limun, Sarolangun Regency, Jambi | Hoabinhian,Neolithic
COMMENTS: Description: Lumut Cave is located on the foot of Calau Tengah Hill. In total, 5 images have been discovered in this cave, comprising geometric lines and planes drawn in black. The images are located around 0.8 m above the modern cave floor. | Keywords: sumatra, hoabinh, neolithic | Sub-cultural Period: Palaeometallic | Alternative_Name: Gua Lumut

LOCALISATION: Sumatra | Napal Melintang, Limun, Sarolangun Regency, Jambi | Hoabinhian,Neolithic
COMMENTS: Description: Aur Duri Cave is located on the foot of Calau Tengah Hill. This cave contains only 1 image, which is in poor condition and comprise a black amorphous design. This faded image is located on the ceiling, about 1.8 m above the modern cave floor. | Keywords: sumatra, hoabinh, neolithic | Sub-cultural Period: Palaeometallic | Alternative_Name: Gua Aur Duri

LOCALISATION: Sumatra | Napal Melintang, Limun, Sarolangun Regency, Jambi | Hoabinhian,Neolithic
COMMENTS: Description: Kasai Cave is located on the foot of Calau Tengah Hill. In total, 7 images have been identified and comprise an anthropomorphic figure, geometric lines, and geometric planes. All images are drawn in black. They images are located around 1-2 m above the modern cave floor. Unfortunately, modern vandalism has obscured several of the drawings. | Keywords: sumatra, hoabinh, neolithic | Sub-cultural Period: Palaeometallic | Alternative_Name: Gua Kasai

LOCALISATION: Sumatra | Napal Melintang, Limun, Sarolangun Regency, Jambi | Hoabinhian,Neolithic
COMMENTS: Description: Pondok Batu is a small rockshelter situated on a cliff of Calau Tengah Hill. It contains 12 images made with a black pigment, and comprise phytomorph, geometric lines, and geometric planes. The images are located on the cave walls around 1.8 m above the modern ground surface. | Keywords: sumatra, hoabinh, neolithic | Sub-cultural Period: Palaeometallic

LOCALISATION: Southeast Sulawesi | Muna Island
COMMENTS: Description: Currently, the rock art has deteriorated off as it is positioned in an open space. There are seven images colored in brown using the brush technique. The images include human figures, soldiers, horse rider and with an escort, and three other images could not be identified.

LOCALISATION: Southeast Sulawesi | Muna Island
COMMENTS: Description: In Muna language, the name of the Kabori Cave means: a cave with writings (or drawings). According to Kosasih (1984), there are 160 rock artworks in the Kabori Cave, while Laode Aksa (1991) had identified 125 drawings that are divided into two groups namely, the northern panel (98 drawings) and the southern panel (27 drawings). Meanwhile, the Makassar Office for Cultural Heritage Conservation recorded 76 rock artworks in 2014. The rock art from the exterior is positioned on the left wall of the cave opening and on the ceiling of the cave opening to the right. In the inner part of the cave, the rock art is spread along the cave wall and most of them are drawn on the left side of the cave wall. As many as 24 types of drawings are identified such as figures of humans (16), hunters mounting on horses (7), hunters without horses (12), soldiers on horseback (5), on-foot soldiers (17), horse-riders (18), crocodile hunters (3), flying men (9), figures of humans (damaged, 4), unfinished drawings of soldiers (1) unfinished drawings of soldiers on horses (2), an abstract human (1), humans fighting (2), a man on horseback with an escort on foot (1), dancers (8), horses (4), deer (2), speared deer (2), dogs (2), a large lizard (1), boats (14), damaged boats (2), sun (1), and 24 other drawings that could not be identified. In addition, there is one image of a negative hand stencil on the right side of the cave wall that was identified in 2015. From the outside, in the upper right hand part of the cave, there are two figures of a man fighting, both wearing soldier outfits holding weapons on the left and right hand, and another figure that is unfinished. Entering the cave, on the right hand side of the cave wall there is a boat with a “linggi” (a boat with horn-shaped bow and stern) that is drawn with an outline technique showing a passenger on the boat, and a crocodile hunter. In the center wall of the cave, the drawings show two scenes of men spearing a deer, a boat, a black centipede, and horseback rider. On the left wall of the cave, there are two sail boats with passengers and a steering device, the drawings of humans are more varied on the left wall of the cave ranging from soldiers holding weapons, soldiers on horseback holding weapons, men piercing a deer with a spear, flying man, and a boat with passengers but with no sail. Different from the rock art in the Metanduno Cave, the rock art depicting animals in the Kabori Cave are generally smaller. Furthermore, the drawings of the boats are quite varied, ranging from drawings of boats with passengers and oars and a steering rod and a sail that are not apparent in drawings in the Metanduno Cave. The negative hand stencils that were drawn using the spraying technique in the site are positioned on the right side of the cave wall near the column pillar, but the remaining images only showed two knuckles of a hand. The drawings in the Metanduno Cave, were quite different from the drawings in the Kabori Cave that sowed negative hand stencils with normal fingers and no ornaments. In general, the drawing technique applied was the line brush technique to create the images of man and animal, while the images of the speared deer and boat used the outline and solid infill. The dominant colors of the drawings are brown, with shades of black and red, but the drawings have mostly faded away and some drawings were vandalized with graffiti on the wall. | Alternative_Name: Liang Kabori

LOCALISATION: Southeast Sulawesi | Muna Island
COMMENTS: Description: This niche was found in 1996 by La Hada, the caretaker of the Kabori Cave Complex, along with the other 18 rock art images on the cave wall. The rock art images were generally drawn using the fingers to form lines or outlines with brown coloring that were positioned at 30 cm to 150 cm above the niche floor. The dominant images that were depicted included human figures, followed by geometrical shapes. In this site there were 10 images of human figures, which among them showed two images that were identified as soldiers similar to the figures of soldiers in the cave of Metanduno and Kabori. Another famous drawing from this site is the image of a human figure playing a kite that was drawn 120 cm from the niche floor and was visible from the niche opening. The drawing of this kite is now used as an icon for the annual kite festival in Muna District.

LOCALISATION: Southeast Sulawesi | Muna Island
COMMENTS: Description: Visible from the footpath heading to Pominsa Cave, there were five rock art images on the first chamber wall consisting of cow-horn shaped boats with a mast on the left, two human figures facing each other, and one human figure facing the right on the lower part of the image. Below the human figures there was one geometric motif of a comb or animal figure. On the exterior wall, there were human figures facing to their right side, drawn on the lower part of the wall. On the exterior walls, there were images of human figures standing akimbo facing the left with both legs slightly curved. Inside the niche, there were at least another 27 images on the cave wall, including images of humans riding some kind of animal, figures of soldiers also showing akimbo position with their hands on their hips facing the right and several triangular shapes facing opposite of each other but there were also several unfinished drawings of the body parts of soldiers. In addition to these drawings, there were several images of soldiers facing one another or some were standing individually with arms akimbo and one arm stretched to the front. There were also figures of some kind of four-legged animal. Still inside the chamber which leads to the second chamber, there is a drawing of a flying human figure with the head facing downwards on the left side, while on the right side, there is a drawing of a human figure riding a horse facing the right. On another rock boulder, there are images of two boats each with a single mast, with a figure of a soldier holding the right side of one of the boats. The images were painted in brown using the finger painting technique. Unfortunately, this rock art site has been spoiled by vandalistic graffiti found in the chambers, as well as some charcoal sketches of human figures.

LOCALISATION: Southeast Sulawesi | Muna Island
COMMENTS: Description: Pominsa Cave was first observed by the Makassar Archeology Office in 2005 and was then documented by the Makassar Preservation of Cultural Heritage Office (BPCB) in 2014. On the cave exterior, there were imprinted rock art images that consisted of negative handprints, human figures, animal figures, kites and geometric shapes. Upon the discovery of these handprints in this site in 2005 by the Makassar Archeology Office, the opinion that there were no negative handprint images on Muna Island was rebuffed. Inside the cave, various images of human figures in a standing position, riding animals, or on a boat, holding weapons, and other geometric images were also found. Most of these images were printed on the wall at a height of 30 cm up to 200 cm from the cave floor using the brush technique and brown coloring. In addition to these images, there were other images that were drawn with black charcoal sketches. | Alternative_Name: Gua Pominsa

LOCALISATION: Southeast Sulawesi | Muna Island
COMMENTS: Description: Most of the rock artworks discovered in this cave are brown in color, using a brush technique that created images of man, horseback riders, soldiers, horses, deer, boats, the sun and other abstract objects, however, the drawings have been tampered and vandalized with graffiti by visitors. | Alternative_Name: Gua La Kolumbu

LOCALISATION: Southeast Sulawesi | Kondongia, Lohia, Muna Regency, South East Sulawesi | Muna Island
COMMENTS: Description: Most of the rock artworks are colored dark brown using the brush technique depicting humans (24 drawings), horse riders (19 drawings), coconut trees, and corn crops. The drawings of the two crops indicate that the community associated to these drawings already have some understanding of agriculture or cultivation. | Alternative_Name: Gua Toko

LOCALISATION: Southeast Sulawesi | Kotano Wuna, Tongkuno, Muna Regency, South East Sulawesi | Muna Island
COMMENTS: Description: The rock art in this cave are mostly colored in brown, using the brush technique showing figures of men in a straddle position, animal figures and other images that were unidentifiable. However, many of the drawings have exfoliated as they are directly exposed to the sunlight. | Alternative_Name: Ceruk La Podo

COMMENTS: Description: The rock art in Andomo Cave was first discovered during an archeology survey in 2011. Another research was conducted in 2013 to re-identify the rock art forms and to further study the variant of rock art motifs, and to recapitulate the total number of the rock art images. In 2012, there were only three panels of rock art that could be identified (the information on the location of the hand stencils in picture 1), while in 2013, there were 5 panels that could be re-identified. Based on the description of the types of the objects, the printed images were generally stencils of the hands, the feet, and other unrecognized stencils. There were 55 images found in Andomo Cave on 5 panels, which were spread all over the upper chamber. These images applied the engraving techniques and were commonly colored in red. The documentation of panel 1 up to panel 5 was sequenced starting from the most western part of the area to the east. The rock art images were positioned in an adequately sunlight-exposed area. There were 42 rock art images that applied the spray technique using red and black colors printed on the right and left wall of the western part of the cave, but unfortunately most of the images have peeled off. The depicted motifs consisted of hand stencils (33 drawings), remnants of hand stencils (5 drawings), foot stencils (1 drawing), and unrecognized images (3 drawings) that were positioned approximately 1 meter to 1.5 meters from the cave floor. Panel 1 has the largest number of images in Andomo Cave consisting of 13 hand stencils that were imprinted using the finger-point technique while the other 29 drawings applied the normal technique. A total of 23 images were identified as using the fingers up to the hand palm ranging from five fingers to three fingers, and 13 images used only the finger-point technique. However, there were no hand stencil images that were drawn using the fingers up to the upper arms. The orientation of the hand stencils varied, with ten images that were oriented upwards, six images were oriented downward, nine images were printed facing the left, and twelve images were drawn facing the right. In addition, three images were identified as the left hand and there was one image depicting the right hand. In panel 2, two rock art images were imprinted, consisting of a foot stencil (?) and one unidentified image colored in red using the spray technique. Meanwhile, in panel 3, the rock art was drawn around 1.15 m above the cave floor, consisting of 5 images that were found with four hand stencil images but one image could not be identified, however all of them were colored in red using the spray technique. One hand stencil was printed using the finger-pointed style facing the left, showing the fingers up to the palm. In panel 3, the hand stencil showed an image of the wrist facing left, which was not found in panel 1. On panel 4, there were only remains of unidentified red shape images that were imprinted using the spray technique. As for panel 5, there were five red hand stencils that also used the spray technique, with three images only consisted of the fingers pointing upwards while the other two images showed fingers up to the palm pointing downwards. There was one image that was printed using the pointed finger style.

LOCALISATION: Southeast Sulawesi | Tira Wonua, Routa, Konawe Regency, South East Sulawesi | Towuti
COMMENTS: Description: The site consists of a niche located on a limestone range which measures 21 meters wide, 5 meters high and around 8 meters inward. The findings from the cave floor consisted of human skulls and bone fragments, ceramic fragments from China and Europe, bronze earrings, and pottery pieces. Three green hand stencil images were found on the ceiling of the niche (LPA 2012) Based on the survey report on 2012, the artifacts found in Lampetia Cave Site included porcelain shards, ceramics, human bones and beads. In 2012, an excavation was conducted to open two test pits (A and B) and during this excavation, the researchers also explored the possibility of identifying rock art in this site. The observation covered the entire cave wall and several rock art images that consisted of hand stencils were found but the images had extremely worn-off. The imprinted rock art was located in a sun-exposed area. The three hand stencil findings were located on the upper wall and the west side of the excavation pit. In identifying the rock art, the images were divided into two panels. Panel 1 consisted of one stencil that depicted four fingers up to the palm, but not decorated, and oriented upwards, colored greenish black and using the spray technique. The images were imprinted 2 meters from the cave floor. Panel 2 consisted of two hand stencils that were oriented upwards with the first image on top. The hand stencils consisted of three fingers oriented upwards that were still visible, and were colored greenish black using the spray technique.

COMMENTS: Description: The surface of the niche wall is green mixed with black. The condition of the image in certain parts is solidly concentrated and some are not.

LOCALISATION: Maluku | Kiltai, Seram Timur, East Seram, Maluku | Seram
COMMENTS: Description: The motif displayed was dark red using ochre material (hematite) using brush and spray techniques, the type of painting is in the form of dots, lines, outlines, solids, and negatives. The identified rock art specifically, i.e., human figure, negative hand stencil, boat motif, geometric motif, vertical stripe motif, fish motif, and remnants of unidentified motifs. Many rock arts were faded out, some were covered in moss, insect nests, exposed to water flow, and flaking of the cliff wall. On Watu Sika cliff, the remnants of insect nests were yellowish red, and the natural rocks were red were due to the natural process on the cliff wall which can interfere rock art identification. However, as the materials used for rock art on cliff wall were different, so the rock art motif could be identified, and the remaining of the insect nest and the red color of the rock could be distinguished. Based on the results of the analysis it is known that there are 66 rock art motifs on this site, consisting of 10.5% of figurative motifs, 25.7% of non-figurative motifs, and the total of unidentified motifs were 63.8 % of the remaining motifs. It used spray techniques of 45 motifs predominantly, brush techniques as many as 20 motifs, and combination of brush and spray technique was 1 motif. The height of the wall panels of rock art from the terrace surface of the cliff up to the ceiling ranged from about 0.7 to 2.5 meters. Rock art panels on the wall of Watu Sika Cliff could be seen by standing on a boat or sitting on a cliff terrace to see the rock art motifs at a close range.

LOCALISATION: Maluku | Buano Utara, Huamual Belakang, West Seram, Maluku | Buano
COMMENTS: Description: The rock art niche is located about 80 meters above sea level. This rock art niche is located on a pretty high cliff wall, so it is relatively dangerous to climb it. Rock climbing types of equipment are required as an essential requirement of safety standards to reach the rock art niche. The characteristics of Austronesian rock art motifs are also similarly evident in the Hatupatola site and the Tanjung Bintang site, such as anthropomorphic, geometric, sun, and circle motifs, as well as being in a difficult to reach position, relatively high cliff face. Based on all the motifs that can still be observed, most of the shapes are still relatively looks clear, but some of the motifs appear to have faded, and there are also star motifs that are almost entirely eroded. In the sun-ray shape motif, apart from having faded in some parts, there are also indications of vandalism over the pigment in the core of the circle. One of the unique features of the sun-ray motif rock art at the Tanjung Bintang Site is the appearance of green pigments. Based on recorded and confirmed archaeological data so far, the green pigment in rock art in Maluku has never been found before.

LOCALISATION: Nusa Tenggara Timur NTT | Wakapsir, Alor Barat Laut, Alor Regency, East Nusa Tenggara | Alor
COMMENTS: Description: Tron Bon Lei is comprised of two adjoining shelters located within a series of shelters in a vol-canic ridge which runs along the south coast of Alor, near Lerabain village. Excavations in 2014 documented a large amount of vertebrate and invertebrate fauna, lithics, shell ornaments, and fish-hooks which occurred as grave goods. Human remains were documented in two of the test pits excavated in the two adjoining shelters in 2014. The cranial remains from square C (TLC-1) were dated using OSL to c.17kya BP. A complete chronometric sequence from the original excavations in Tron Bon Lei identi-fied four phases of human occupations: Late Holocene (c.2.7–5.4kya cal BP), middle Holocene(c.7.4–8.2kya cal BP), early Holocene-terminal Pleistocene (c.9.4–12.7kya cal BP), and LatePleistocene (c.17.4-21kya cal BP). Commonalities between Mainland and Island Southeast Asian mortuary treatments begin-ning in the Late Pleistocene, with a common custom of flexed burial as seen in Tron Bon Lei(TLB-1), suggest transmission of ideas. In Tron Bon Lei, rock art occurs in both sections of the overhang (Tron Bon Lei 1 and 2) where the shelter walls protect a flat floor deposit. As the shelters are part of a continuous overhang they are discussed here simply as Tron Bon Lei. Anthropomorphs are the most common figurative motifs found at Tron Bon Lei followed by ‘boats’. Tron Bon Lei also contains red hand prints. Anthropomorphs are the most common figurative motif at Tron Bon Lei with at least ten recorded. All are in red pigment. The head in all figures is shown in solid red pigment. Some figures appear to be holding weapons. one wielding an ‘axe’ and another one has a possible club and a ‘shield’. The anthropomorphs on the left of the panel appear to be wearing headdresses of a fan-like shape. | Keywords: alor

LOCALISATION: Nusa Tenggara Timur NTT | Wakapsir, Alor Barat Laut, Alor Regency, East Nusa Tenggara | Alor
COMMENTS: Description: Ba Lei is a complex of three small caves about 300 m to the west of Tron Bon Lei. Ba Lei 2 the second largest cave in the complex had no obvious rock art but this may be due to poor preservation of pigment on the wall surfaces. Ba Lei 3, the smallest of the three caves is only 2 m high and 4 m wide at its maximum dimension but contained white geometric motifs and some faded red pigment. | Keywords: alor

LOCALISATION: Nusa Tenggara Timur NTT | QF5V+2FF, Bungamuda, Ile Ape, Lembata Regency, East Nusa Tenggara | Lembata
COMMENTS: Description: Rock art at the Nali site was found on a large volcanic boulder in the village of Atawtung-Lamagute, Lamalera district, on the northern slope of the Ile Lewotolok volcano. The boulder belongs to a Holocene vol- canic formation of the still-active volcano Ile Lewotolok (Koesoemadi- nata and Noya, 1990). The rock art at the Nali site shows a standing anthropomorphic figure comprising subimages of hands, fingers, feet, and toes (Fig. 1b). O’Connor and coworkers (2018b) also identified other subimages, such as male genitalia, a protrusion on both sides of the head, and a head adornment at the center of the head. Rock art at the Nali site possesses a distinctive characteristic that is uncommon in the corpus of rock art in East Indonesia and East Timor, this site presents a white-colored static-posed anthropomorphic figure instead of an active-posed human figure drawn with red pigment (Bal- lard, 1988, Ballard, 1992, O’Connor, 2003). Moreover, this rock art was thought to show similarities to the bronze Dong Son drum (Moko drum) image in an ancestral village in Lewohala, East Flores, East Nusa Tenggara (O’Connor et al., 2018b). | Keywords: lembata | Alternative_Name: Wato Nale
